scenarioStory.history: 52-year-old male found by passerby in the alley behind a tavern at 0400 hrs. Ambient temperature 18°F (-7.8°C) with light wind. Last seen leaving the bar at approximately 2300 hrs the night before — roughly 5 hours of exposure. Patient was found supine on cold concrete, dressed in a light jacket and jeans, clothing partially damp. No bystander CPR initiated. On arrival: apneic, no obvious palpable pulse, body cold and rigid throughout. Rectal core temperature (when obtained) 25°C / 77°F — severe hypothermia. Pupils midposition. Cold rigor present (mimics rigor mortis but is reversible). No obvious traumatic injury. Empty liquor bottle near patient.
scenarioStory.course: Initial Contact: EMS arrives in the alley to find a 52-year-old male supine on cold concrete, apneic, body rigid and cold. No bystander CPR. Surroundings are dark, cold, and exposed. The crew must immediately begin a hypothermia-modified primary assessment — including the 60-second pulse check that the protocol specifically requires for suspected hypothermic patients — while simultaneously protecting the patient from further heat loss and preparing for prolonged, gentle resuscitation. → Gentle CPR, one shock: Pulselessness confirmed after 60-second check. Coarse VFib on the monitor. Crew initiates high-quality but gentle chest compressions (rate same as normothermic: 100–120/min, depth 2–2.4 inches), applies defibrillator pads, and delivers ONE defibrillation attempt at 200 J recognizing the patient's core temperature is below 30°C. Patient is being moved with extreme care — the cold heart is electrically irritable and rough handling can re-trigger VF. Per the protocol, no further shocks will be attempted until the core temperature rises above 30°C (86°F) or the patient has been rewarmed at least 1–2°C. → Warming, extended ACLS: Patient is in the warmed ambulance compartment with CPR ongoing. Single hypothermia-protocol defibrillation already delivered without conversion. The crew now runs concurrent aggressive warming and extended-interval ACLS: warmed IV crystalloid bolus, large heat packs with barrier on anterior chest/torso (NOT extremities — cold peripheral blood return triggers VF), forced-air warming if available, cabin heat maxed. Per the Alaska/Wilderness Medical Society interpretation in the hypothermia protocol, medications are withheld below 30°C; if epinephrine is given per AHA, the interval is doubled (every 6–10 min, not q3–5). No further defibrillation attempts will be made until core temperature has risen above 30°C / 86°F. Long transport to ECMO-capable receiving facility is underway. Resuscitation will continue for the duration of transport — patients can survive intact even after prolonged downtime when hypothermic. → ROSC after rewarming: Sustained CPR and concurrent warming have raised the core temperature above 30°C. A second defibrillation attempt at 200 J converts the rhythm to a slow sinus bradycardia with a faint, palpable carotid pulse. The patient remains unresponsive, GCS 3, with a weak BP. Post-ROSC care begins: continue warming toward 35°C, secure airway if not already, gentle ventilation guided by EtCO2, support BP, and continue to the ECMO/rewarming-capable facility. Drug intervals remain doubled until 35°C is reached.

patientInformation.patientCondition: Unresponsive, pulseless after prolonged cold exposure

scenarioEvents.name: Initial Contact
scenarioEvents.description: EMS arrives in the alley to find a 52-year-old male supine on cold concrete, apneic, body rigid and cold. No bystander CPR. Surroundings are dark, cold, and exposed. The crew must immediately begin a hypothermia-modified primary assessment — including the 60-second pulse check that the protocol specifically requires for suspected hypothermic patients — while simultaneously protecting the patient from further heat loss and preparing for prolonged, gentle resuscitation.
relatedChecklist: Confirm scene safety; PD secured; mind icy surface and ambient cold
Primary survey with extended 60-second pulse check (cold = slow circulation)
Recognize severe hypothermia (cold body, rigid, midpos pupils) — not rigor mortis
Rule out resuscitation contraindications (no ice in airway, chest depressible, no fatal injury)
Extricate gently to warmed ambulance compartment (≥24°C / 75°F)

scenarioEvents.name: Gentle CPR, one shock
scenarioEvents.description: Pulselessness confirmed after 60-second check. Coarse VFib on the monitor. Crew initiates high-quality but gentle chest compressions (rate same as normothermic: 100–120/min, depth 2–2.4 inches), applies defibrillator pads, and delivers ONE defibrillation attempt at 200 J recognizing the patient's core temperature is below 30°C. Patient is being moved with extreme care — the cold heart is electrically irritable and rough handling can re-trigger VF. Per the protocol, no further shocks will be attempted until the core temperature rises above 30°C (86°F) or the patient has been rewarmed at least 1–2°C.
relatedChecklist: Handle gently; keep horizontal; minimize extremity motion (irritable cold heart)
Compressions 100–120/min, depth 2–2.4 in, full recoil — same rate as normothermic
Obtain core temperature (rectal in warm cabin, or esophageal if intubated)
Defibrillate ONCE at 200 J; no more shocks until core temp >30°C
BVM at 10/min during CPR; do NOT hyperventilate (hypocarbia lowers VF threshold)
Cut off wet clothing (do NOT pull); dry skin; insulate with vapor barrier and blanket

scenarioEvents.name: Warming, extended ACLS
scenarioEvents.description: Patient is in the warmed ambulance compartment with CPR ongoing. Single hypothermia-protocol defibrillation already delivered without conversion. The crew now runs concurrent aggressive warming and extended-interval ACLS: warmed IV crystalloid bolus, large heat packs with barrier on anterior chest/torso (NOT extremities — cold peripheral blood return triggers VF), forced-air warming if available, cabin heat maxed. Per the Alaska/Wilderness Medical Society interpretation in the hypothermia protocol, medications are withheld below 30°C; if epinephrine is given per AHA, the interval is doubled (every 6–10 min, not q3–5). No further defibrillation attempts will be made until core temperature has risen above 30°C / 86°F. Long transport to ECMO-capable receiving facility is underway. Resuscitation will continue for the duration of transport — patients can survive intact even after prolonged downtime when hypothermic.
relatedChecklist: Warmed isotonic crystalloid IV bolus (ideally 42°C / 107.6°F)
Large heat packs (with barrier) on anterior chest/torso — NOT extremities
WITHHOLD further defibrillation until core temp >30°C / 86°F
Withhold pressors <30°C, or extend epi to q6–10 min (NOT q3–5) per protocol
Epinephrine 1 mg IV (1:10,000) q6–10 min if given — doubled interval per hypothermia rules
Continuous EtCO2 to verify compression quality and detect ROSC
Transport to ECMO/cardiopulmonary bypass-capable center; continue CPR en route
Continue resus; do NOT terminate for hypothermia (not dead until warm and dead)

scenarioEvents.name: ROSC after rewarming
scenarioEvents.description: Sustained CPR and concurrent warming have raised the core temperature above 30°C. A second defibrillation attempt at 200 J converts the rhythm to a slow sinus bradycardia with a faint, palpable carotid pulse. The patient remains unresponsive, GCS 3, with a weak BP. Post-ROSC care begins: continue warming toward 35°C, secure airway if not already, gentle ventilation guided by EtCO2, support BP, and continue to the ECMO/rewarming-capable facility. Drug intervals remain doubled until 35°C is reached.
relatedChecklist: Transition to Post-ROSC care; continue active rewarming en route
Ventilate 10/min via SGA; titrate to normal EtCO2; do NOT hyperventilate
Keep doubled drug intervals until core temp >35°C / 95°F
Early notification to ECMO-capable receiving facility

scenarioEvents.name: Stacked shocks at <30°C
scenarioEvents.description: BRANCH (judgment error). Crew runs standard normothermic ACLS without recognizing the hypothermic context. Three stacked 200 J defibrillation attempts are delivered with the patient's core temperature at 25°C. None convert. The cold myocardium is refractory to electricity and now further stressed. Crew has expended capacitor charge, time, and provider attention while skipping the actual intervention — warming. Coarse VFib has degraded to fine VFib. EtCO2 is borderline. Teaching point: 'At <30°C, one shock and that's it. Then warm. Cold hearts don't convert.'
relatedChecklist: Recognize error; STOP further shocks; obtain core temp; pivot to warming
Move to warm cabin; remove wet clothes; warmed IV fluid; torso heat packs

scenarioEvents.name: Premature termination
scenarioEvents.description: BRANCH (judgment error). Crew applies the standard non-traumatic termination of resuscitation criteria after roughly 20 minutes of CPR with persistent VF/asystole and EtCO2 < 20 mmHg. Online medical control is contacted. The hypothermia exclusion in the termination protocol is missed. Resuscitation is stopped. The patient — who at this point has been resuscitated cold and never warmed past 25–26°C — is pronounced dead in the field. Teaching point: the termination protocol explicitly excludes hypothermia. 'Not dead until warm and dead.' This patient had a recoverable rhythm and a recoverable brain.
relatedChecklist: Recognize hypothermia is an EXCLUSION from termination criteria
Note: termination requires 'no evidence or suspicion of hypothermia' — does not apply here
Resume CPR and warming; contact medical control; transport to ECMO center

Extricate gently to warmed ambulance compartment (≥24°C / 75°F)

Obtain core temperature (rectal in warm cabin, or esophageal if intubated)
Defibrillate ONCE at 200 J; no more shocks until core temp >30°C
BVM at 10/min during CPR; do NOT hyperventilate (hypocarbia lowers VF threshold)
Cut off wet clothing (do NOT pull); dry skin; insulate with vapor barrier and blanket
Warmed isotonic crystalloid IV bolus (ideally 42°C / 107.6°F)
Large heat packs (with barrier) on anterior chest/torso — NOT extremities
WITHHOLD further defibrillation until core temp >30°C / 86°F
Withhold pressors <30°C, or extend epi to q6–10 min (NOT q3–5) per protocol
Epinephrine 1 mg IV (1:10,000) q6–10 min if given — doubled interval per hypothermia rules
Continuous EtCO2 to verify compression quality and detect ROSC
Transport to ECMO/cardiopulmonary bypass-capable center; continue CPR en route
Continue resus; do NOT terminate for hypothermia (not dead until warm and dead)
Transition to Post-ROSC care; continue active rewarming en route
Ventilate 10/min via SGA; titrate to normal EtCO2; do NOT hyperventilate
Keep doubled drug intervals until core temp >35°C / 95°F
Early notification to ECMO-capable receiving facility
Recognize error; STOP further shocks; obtain core temp; pivot to warming
Move to warm cabin; remove wet clothes; warmed IV fluid; torso heat packs
Recognize hypothermia is an EXCLUSION from termination criteria
Note: termination requires 'no evidence or suspicion of hypothermia' — does not apply here
Resume CPR and warming; contact medical control; transport to ECMO center
